# Concept Card: Security-RBAC

## Definition
Transcript evidence থেকে derived canonical definition; conflicting views থাকলে সব variant রাখা হয়েছে।

## First Principles
System boundary, control loop, এবং failure behavior দৃষ্টিতে concept ব্যাখ্যা করতে হবে।

## Mental Model
এই concept-কে runtime contract হিসেবে ধরলে debugging ও design decisions সহজ হয়।

## Why It Exists
Operational complexity কমানো এবং reliable automation enable করা।

## Internal Working
Control plane state reconciliation + node-level execution pathway।

## YAML and Command Usage
Related raw commands এবং manifests reference-based রাখা হয়েছে; no rewrite of original evidence.

## Use Cases
Lab, production-like demos, and certification patterns থেকে mapped।

## Edge Cases
Version mismatch, policy conflict, scheduling/storage/network constraints উল্লেখ করা হয়েছে।

## Pitfalls
Warning/Pitfall entries evidence সহ retain করা হয়েছে।

## Troubleshooting Angle
Symptom -> Cause -> Fix logic chain evidence-based maintain করা হয়েছে।

## Exam Relevance
CKA/CKAD speed + correctness + troubleshooting expectations mapped।

### Evidence 55: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/082_(2025 Updates)Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E31
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 082_(2025 Updates)Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The default storage class admission controller that observes the creation of PVCs and automatically adds the default storage class to them if one is not specified, the event rate limit admission controller can help set a limit on the requests with the the API server can handle at a time.
- Multi-concept tags: Storage, Security-RBAC, Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 152: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The default storage class admission controller will watch for requests to create a PVC and check if it has a storage class mentioned in it.
- Multi-concept tags: Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 153: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E10

- Multi-concept tags: Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 154: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E11

### Evidence 155: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Implementation Step

### Evidence 156: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E13

### Evidence 157: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It can change or mutate the object itself before it is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 158: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So those are two types of admission controllers.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 159: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Mutating admission controllers are those that can change the request, and validating admission controllers are those that can validate the request and allow or deny it.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 160: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And there may be admission controllers that can do both that can mutate a request as well as validate a request.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 161: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, generally mutating admission controllers are invoked first, followed by validating admission controllers.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 162: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is so that any change made by the mutating admission controller can be considered during the validation process.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 163: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this example, the namespace auto provisioning admission controller, which is a mutating admission controller, is run first, followed by the validating controller namespace exists.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 164: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If it was run the other way, then the namespace exists.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 165: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Admission controller would always reject the request for namespace that does not exist, and the namespace auto provisioning controller would never be invoked to create the namespace.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 166: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 085_(2025 Updates)Validating and Mutating Admission Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And when a request goes through these admission controllers, if any admission controller rejects the request, the request is rejected and an error message is shown to the user.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 694: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/05_Application Lifecycle Management/127_(2025 Updates) Vertical Pod Autoscaling (VPA)_.extraction.md::E33

### Evidence 811: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, when a user tries to access a web server, TLS certificates ensure that the communication between the user and the server is encrypted and the server is who it says it is.

### Evidence 812: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt

- Content: Let's take a look at a scenario without secure connectivity.

### Evidence 813: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If a user were to access his online banking application, the credentials he types in would be sent in a plain text format.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 814: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E6

- Content: Well, that's obviously not safe, so you must encrypt the data being transferred using encryption keys.

### Evidence 816: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt

### Evidence 817: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You add the random number to your data, and you encrypt it into a format that cannot be recognized.

### Evidence 818: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Implementation Step

### Evidence 819: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The hacker sniffing the network gets the data but can't do anything with it.

### Evidence 820: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, the same is the case with the server receiving the data.

### Evidence 821: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It cannot decrypt the data without the key.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 822: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So a copy of the key must also be sent to the server so that the server can decrypt and read the message.

### Evidence 823: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Since the key is also sent over the same network, the attacker can sniff that as well and decrypt the data with it.

### Evidence 824: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E16

- Content: This is known as symmetric encryption.

### Evidence 825: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E17

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 147_TLS Basics.txt

- Content: It is a secure way of encryption, but since it uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt the data, and since the key has to be exchanged between the sender and the receiver.

### Evidence 828: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/147_TLS Basics.extraction.md::E20

### Evidence 1919: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/162_Authorization.extraction.md::E8

- Content: In this lecture, we will look at the various concepts related to security in Docker.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2605: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let us start with a host with Docker installed on it.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2606: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This host has a set of its own processes running, such as a number of operating system processes, the Docker daemon itself, the SSH server, etc. we will now run an Ubuntu Docker container that runs a process that sleeps for an hour.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2607: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We have learned that unlike virtual machines, containers are not completely isolated from their host containers, and the hosts share the same kernel.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, On-Prem

### Evidence 2608: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Containers are isolated using namespaces.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2609: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In Linux, the host has a namespace and the containers have their own namespace.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2610: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: All the processes run by the containers are in fact run on the host itself, but in their own namespace.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2611: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As far as the Docker container is concerned, it is in its own namespace and it can see its own processes only.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2612: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It cannot see anything outside of it or in any other namespace.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2613: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So when you list the processes from within the Docker container, you see the sleep process with a process ID of one for the Docker host, all processes of its own, as well as those in the child.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2614: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Namespaces are visible as just another process in the system.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 2615: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So when you list the processes on the host, you see a list of processes, including the sleep command, but with a different process ID.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 2616: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 175_Pre-requisite - Security in Docker.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is because the processes can have different process IDs in different namespaces.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

- Content: Similarly, in case of the back end API server, it receives ingress traffic from the web server on port 5000 and has egress traffic to port 3306 to the database server, and from the database servers perspective, it receives ingress traffic on port 3306 from the API server.

### Evidence 2731: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/179_Network Policy.extraction.md::E15

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If we were to list the rules required to get this working, we would have an ingress rule that is required to accept HTTP traffic on port 80 on the web server, and egress rule to allow traffic from the web server to port 5000 on the API server, and ingress rule to accept traffic on port 5000 on the API server, and an egress rule to allow traffic to port 3306 on the database server.
- Multi-concept tags: Networking, Security-RBAC, Architecture, Platform Engineering

### Evidence 3058: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/184_(2025 Updates)Custorm Resource Definition (CRD).extraction.md::E13

- Type: Implementation Step

- Content: However, developing a controller in Python may be challenging as the calls made to the APIs may become expensive and we will need to create our own queuing and caching mechanisms.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3177: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/186_(2025 Updates) Custom Controllers.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 186_(2025 Updates) Custom Controllers.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Developing in go with the Kubernetes Go client provides support for other libraries like shared informers, that provide caching and queuing mechanisms that can help build controllers easily and in the right way.

### Evidence 3272: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, even a relatively simple WordPress site might need the following, a deployment to deploy the pods that you wanna run, such as MySQL database servers or web servers, a persistent volume to store the database, a persistent volume claim, a service to expose the web server running in a pod to the internet, a secret to store credentials like admin passwords and other things, and maybe even more if you want extra stuff like periodic backups, jobs and so on.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC, Configuration

- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 3276: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E20

### Evidence 3277: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E22

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Furthermore, with RBAC, security is much improved and any user can be limited in what they can do with Helm.

### Evidence 3278: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And before, you had to set these limits in Tiller and that was not the best option, but with RBAC built from ground up to fine-tune user permissions in Kubernetes, it's now straightforward to do.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 3279: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E24

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As far as Kubernetes is concerned, it doesn't matter if the user is trying to make changes within the cluster with kubectl or with Helm commands, the user requesting the changes has the same RBAC-allowed permissions, whatever tool they use.

### Evidence 3916: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E32

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But what if your third-party application is hosted on the Kubernetes cluster itself?

### Evidence 3918: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, we can have our custom Kubernetes dashboard application, or the Prometheus application deployed on the Kubernetes cluster itself.

### Evidence 3919: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E35

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In that case, this whole process of exporting the service account token and configuring the third-party application to use it can be made simple by automatically mounting the service token secret as a volume inside the pod hosting the third-party application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3920: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E36

### Evidence 3923: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E39
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For every namespace in Kubernetes, a service account named default is automatically created.

### Evidence 3924: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E40
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Each namespace has its own default service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3925: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E41
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Whenever a pod is created, the default service account and its token are automatically mounted to that pod as a volume mount.

- Content: For example, we have a simple pod definition file that creates a pod using my custom Kubernetes dashboard image.

### Evidence 3927: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E43

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Content: However, when the pod is created, if you look at the details of the pod, by running the kubectl describe pod command, you see that a volume is automatically created from the secret named default token, which is in fact the secret containing the token for this default service account.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 3934: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E50
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If you'd like to use a different service account such as the one we just created, modify the pod definition file to include a service account field and specify the name of the new service account.

### Evidence 3935: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Remember, you cannot edit the service account of an existing pod.

### Evidence 3936: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E52
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You must delete and recreate the pod.

### Evidence 3937: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E53
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, in case of a deployment, you will be able to edit the service account as any changes to the pod definition file will automatically trigger a new rollout for the deployment.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the deployment will take care of deleting and recreating new pods with the right service account.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

### Evidence 3940: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E56
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So remember, Kubernetes automatically mounts the default service account if you haven't explicitly specified any.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3941: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E57
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You may choose not to mount a service account automatically by setting the automountServiceAccountToken field to false in the pod spec section.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3942: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E58
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let's now discuss some of the changes that were made in releases version 1.22 and 1.24 of Kubernetes that changed the way service accounts secrets and tokens worked.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3943: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E59
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, as we discussed in the previous video, every namespace has a default service account, and that service account has a secret object with a token associated with it.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3944: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E60
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When a pod is created, it automatically associates the service account to the pod and mounts the token to a well-known location within the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3945: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E61
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, it's under var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3946: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E62
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This makes the token accessible to a process that's running within the pod, and that enables that process to query the Kubernetes API.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

### Evidence 3951: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E67

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

### Evidence 3952: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E68

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now let's take that token that we just saw, and if you decode this token using this command, or you could just copy and paste this token in the JWT website at jwt.io, you'll see that it has no expiry date defined in the payload section here on the right.

### Evidence 3953: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E69
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this is a token that does not have an expiry date set.

### Evidence 3954: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E70
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this excerpt from the Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal for creating bound service account tokens describes this form of JWT to be having some security and scalability related issues.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Troubleshooting, On-Prem

### Evidence 3955: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E71
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the current implementation of JWT is not bound to any audience and is not time bound as we just saw.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3956: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E72
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There was no expiry date for the token.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3957: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E73
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the JWT is valid as long as the service account exists.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3958: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E74
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Moreover, each JWT requires a separate secret object per service account, which results in scalability issues.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 3959: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E75
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And as such, in version 1.22, the TokenRequestAPI was introduced as part of the Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal 1205 that aimed to introduce a mechanism for provisioning Kubernetes service account tokens that are more secure and scalable via an API.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3960: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E76
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So tokens generated by the token request API are audience bound, they're time bound and object bound, and hence are more secure.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3961: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E77
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, since version 1.22, when a new pod is created, it no longer relies on the service account secret token that we just saw.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3962: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E78
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, a token with a defined lifetime is generated through the TokenRequestAPI by the service account admission controller when the pod is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3963: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E79
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And this token is then mount as a projected volume onto the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3964: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E80
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So in the past, if you look at this space here, you'd see the secret that's part of the service account mount as a secret object.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3965: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E81
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But now as you can see, it's a projected volume that actually communicates with the token controller API, TokenRequestAPI, and it gets a token for the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3966: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E82
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now with version 1.24, another enhancement was made as part of the Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal 2799, which dealt with the reduction of secret-based service account tokens.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3967: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E83
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So in the past, when a service account was created, it automatically created a secret with a token that has no expiry and is not bound to any audience.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3968: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E84
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This was then automatically mount as a volume to any pod that uses that service account.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And that's what we just saw.

### Evidence 3970: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E86
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But in version 1.22, that was changed.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3971: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E87
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The automatic mounting of the secret object to the pod was changed, and instead it then moved to the TokenRequestAPI.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3972: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E88
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So with version 1.24, a change was made where when you create a service account, it no longer automatically creates a secret or a token access secret.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3973: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E89
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So you must run the command kubectl create token, followed by the name of the service account to generate a token for that service account if you needed one.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 3974: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E90
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And it will then print that token on screen.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3975: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E91
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, if you copy that token, and then if you try to decode this token, this time, you'll see that it has an expiry date defined.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3976: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E92
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And if you haven't specified any time limit, then it's usually one hour from the time that you run the command.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 3977: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E93
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You can also pass in additional options to the command to increase the expiry of the token.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 3978: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E94
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So now post version 1.24, if you would still like to create secrets the old way with non-expiring token, then you could still do that by creating a secret object with the type set to kubernetes.io/service-account-token and the name of the service account specified within annotations in the metadata section like this.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3979: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E95
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this is how the secret object will be associated with that particular service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3980: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E96
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So when you do this, just make sure that you have the service account created first and then create a secret object.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3981: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E97
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Otherwise, the secret object will not be created.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3982: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E98
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this will create a non-expiring token in a secret object and associated with that service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3983: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E99
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, you have to be sure if you really wanna do that because as per the Kubernetes documentation pages on service account token secrets, it says you should only create service account token secrets if you can't use the TokenRequestAPI to obtain a token.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3984: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E100
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that's either the kubectl create token command we just talked about, or it talks to the TokenRequestAPI to generate that token, or it's the automated token creation that happens on pods when they're created post version 1.22.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 3985: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E101
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And also, you should only create service account token request if the security exposure of persisting and non-expiring token credential is acceptable to you.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 3986: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E102
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, the TokenRequestAPI is recommended instead of using the service account token secret objects as they are more secure and have a bounded lifetime unlike the service account token secrets that have no expiry.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

- Content: As an admin, we're able to perform any operation, but soon we will have others accessing the cluster as well, such as the other administrators, developers, testers, or other applications like monitoring applications or continuous delivery applications like Jenkins, etc. so we will be creating accounts for them to access the cluster by creating usernames and passwords or tokens or signed TLS certificates or service accounts, as we saw in the previous lectures.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Architecture, Observability

- Type: Warning/Pitfall

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But we don't want all of them to have the same level of access as us.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 4411: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/146_Authorization.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Warning/Pitfall

- Content: For example, we don't want the developers to have access to modify our cluster configuration, like adding or deleting nodes or the storage or networking configurations.

- Type: Concept

- Content: And this is part of the rule for a of the Kubernetes deprecation policy.

### Evidence 5273: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E49
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This rule states that other than the most recent API versions in each track older API versions must be supported after they're announced deprecation for a duration of no less than GA 12 months or three releases for GA and nine months or three releases for beta and zero releases for alpha.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5274: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E50
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So alpha versions need not be supported for any release, but beta and GA versions once released must be supported anywhere from nine to 12 months.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5275: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that's why the release x plus one does not have V1 alpha one.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Why isn't the, v one beta two version the preferred and storage version because rule four B states that the preferred API version and the storage version for a given group may not advance until after a release has been made that supports both the new version and the previous version.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt

### Evidence 5308: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E84

### Evidence 5309: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E85
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But now that we have the V two alpha one version ready can we deprecate the V1 version now?
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5310: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E86
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: No, because that's where rule three comes in.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5311: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E87
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And rule three says an API version in a given track may not be deprecated until a new API version, at least as stable is released.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5312: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E88
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Meaning GA versions can deprecate beta or alpha versions but not the other way around.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5313: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E89
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: An alpha version aren't deprecate a GA version in this case the V2 alpha one is an alpha version and V one is a GA version, so we cannot deprecate V one now.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5314: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E90
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The V two alpha one version needs to go through its life cycle of becoming a V two, alpha two then V two beta one, then v2 beta two, and then V two and and only can the V2 version deprecate the V1 version.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC

### Evidence 5315: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/09_Security/160_API Deprecations.extraction.md::E91
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 09_Security / 160_API Deprecations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that brings us to the final topic of this video, the cube converts command.
